title: Spin torque driven mode hybridization and band engineering in nanopatterned magnonic crystals
abstract: Spin wave propagation and dynamic control are essential for reconfigurable magnonic and spintronic devices. Here, tunable mode coupling and band hybridisation are demonstrated in a nanopatterned bicomponent magnonic crystal consisting of a Permalloy/heavy metal bilayer patterned with a two dimensional array of Co nanodots. Using the plane wave method and the linearised Landau Lifshitz equation with a field like torque term, we show that inhomogeneous current induced spin torque produces periodic modulation of the magnonic frequency, enabling dynamic control of spin-wave dispersion. A pronounced avoided crossing between localised and propagating Damon Eshbach modes is observed, leading to tunable hybridisation gaps, band deformation, and enhanced mode mixing. The spin torque induced modulation enables controlled mode conversion and reconfigurable hybrid magnonic states, demonstrating efficient electrical tuning of nanoscale spin-wave dynamics.
summary: This paper demonstrates how electrical current can be used to actively engineer the band structure of a magnonic crystal. By applying inhomogeneous spin-orbit torque, the researchers show they can induce mode hybridization and create tunable band gaps. This provides a pathway for developing programmable, reconfigurable magnonic and spintronic devices.
main_problem: The study addresses the need for active, electrical control over spin-wave propagation and band structures in magnonic crystals, moving beyond passive systems.
main_result: The researchers demonstrate that inhomogeneous current-induced spin-orbit torque can induce band hybridization, create tunable hybridization gaps, and enable the reconfiguration of magnonic states through mode conversion.
method: The study uses the Plane Wave Method (PWM) to solve the linearized Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) equation in the reciprocal lattice, applying perturbation theory to model the periodic spin-torque term.
model_or_system: A 2D bicomponent magnonic crystal consisting of a Permalloy/heavy metal bilayer nanopatterned with a 2D array of Cobalt nanodots, modeled using the LLG equation with a field-like torque term.
key_observables: Spin-wave band structures, magnonic eigenmodes, hybridization gaps, avoided crossings (anticrossing), and mode profiles.
important_parameters: Current density (J), spin-torque scaling factor, saturation magnetization (Ms), lattice constant (a), and torque efficiencies (c_||, c_perp).
main_assumptions: Neglected Gilbert damping and damping-like torque; assumed uniform static magnetization; used a linearized approximation for spin-wave dynamics; neglected magnetocrystalline anisotropy.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the crystal structure and Brillouin Zone; Fig 2 compares dispersion with and without spin torque; Fig 3 shows eigenmode profiles at the Gamma point; Fig 4 shows the evolution of the anticrossing gap; Fig 5 and 6 show the evolution of modes through hybridization.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of active magnonic control, describes the bicomponent crystal system and the LLG-based mathematical model, presents numerical results using the Plane Wave Method regarding band engineering and mode hybridization, and concludes with implications for reconfigurable spintronic devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a)First BZ for the structure shown in (b), with indicate high-symmetry points, Γ, Y , M and Z in the cen- tre and at the border of the first BZ. There are two direc- tions of the wave vector k along the bias magnetic field and perpendicular to it, i.e., the backward volume magnetostatic wave(BVMW) and Damon–Eshbach (DE) geometry, respec- tively. (b)Permalloy thin film interfaced with a heavy metal and patterned with a two-dimensional array of circular Co nanodots, thereby forming a bicomponent magnonic crys- tal(BMC) with spatially induced magnetic and spin-torque parameters. The thickness of the bicomponent magnonic crystal is d, the lattice constant is a. The magnetic field H0 is...

FIG. 2. Magnonic band structure of 2D BMC with heavy metal layer interfaced with Py thin film calculated along the path in the first BZ shown in Fig. 1(a). The device is sat- urated along the z axis by the static external magnetic field µ0H0 = 15mT. The grey-coloured band structure is obtained without considering spin torque effects, and the blue-dotted band structure is obtained by incorporating the spin torque term in the PWM analysis. The capital letters A and B is the region of hybridization. The region A is enlarged in Fig. 4. The eigen mode profile for the point at 1 and 2 is depicted in Fig. 3.
FIG. 3. Magnon band resolved spin-wave eigenmodes profile corresponding to the selected points in the dispersion relation shown in Fig. 2(point 1 and 2).

FIG. 4. Magnonic band structures around the anti-crossings region, The zoomed version of band structure shown in Fig. 2. The coupling factor(g = ∆f/2) versus different spin torque scaling factor is shown as inset. The inset shows how the proposed spin-torque-induced magnonic crystal can be elec- trically tuned.

FIG. 5. Spin wave eigen mod evolution at different point in the dispersion in Fig. 4 under periodic injection of effective spin torque term into magnonic lattice. The white circles show the regular pattern of Co dots placed in Py. The red and blue areas show how the phase and amplitude of the dynamic magnetisation are distributed.
